aiMessages.content: Help me to build a bot that could download video link from tiktok using rapid api tikok then send the video to chatroom

Created a new Telegram TikTok downloader bot using grammY. It auto-detects TikTok links, enforces a per-chat cooldown, calls your RapidAPI downloader endpoint, downloads the mp4 server-side, and sends it as video with fallbacks to document and then a direct link. Added production-safe logging, a runtime Bot Profile string, and DOCS.md plus a clean .env.sample for RapidAPI config.

Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram bot for downloading TikTok videos via RapidAPI and delivering them in chats

Summary:
Build a production-ready Telegram bot (single Node.js process, grammY) that accepts TikTok links, fetches downloadable video data via a RapidAPI TikTok endpoint, downloads the media server-side, and sends it back to the user or group chat. The system must be robust against Telegram file-size limits, TikTok/RapidAPI failures, duplicate requests, and abuse.

The bot will focus on a simple user experience (paste link, receive video) while implementing operational safeguards: request validation, rate limiting, deduplication, timeouts, retries with backoff, and observability. MongoDB is used for persistence (jobs, cache, users, quotas) and a simple filesystem storage or object storage abstraction is used for temporary media files when needed.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Users can paste a TikTok video URL in a private chat, group, or channel (where the bot is present) and receive the video file back in the same chat.

Core user expectations:
1) Minimal friction: no complex commands required; link detection should just work.
2) Fast feedback: immediate acknowledgement, progress messages when appropriate, and a clear success/failure outcome.
3) Reliability: handle intermittent RapidAPI failures, TikTok link variants, and Telegram upload issues.
4) Safety: protect the bot from abuse (spam, very high volume, repeated downloads) and from oversized files that cannot be sent.

Scope decisions to make it production-ready:
1) Supported inputs: standard TikTok share links, mobile links, and shortened URLs (resolve redirects).
2) Supported outputs: video file (preferred), fallback to sending a direct download link if file exceeds Telegram limits or download fails.
3) Chat contexts: private chats and groups by default; optional channel support where the bot has posting rights.
4) Localization: start with one language; design for future localization by centralizing user-facing strings.

Non-goals (initial release):
1) Full media editing (watermark removal variations beyond what the API provides).
2) Multi-tenant admin dashboard.
3) Heavy background processing pipelines or external queues (single-process constraint).

High-level architecture:
High-level components inside a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram update handling layer (grammY): receives messages, commands, and callback queries; routes them to handlers.
2) Input normalization and validation module: detects TikTok URLs, expands short links, extracts canonical video identifier where possible.
3) Job orchestration module: creates and tracks download jobs, enforces deduplication, and limits concurrency.
4) RapidAPI client module: calls the TikTok RapidAPI endpoint with timeouts, retries, and structured error handling.
5) Media download and delivery module: downloads the video to temporary storage or streams it (depending on size and Telegram constraints), then uploads/sends via Telegram API.
6) Persistence layer: MongoDB for users, quotas, job state, and cache of resolved TikTok metadata and file references.
7) Observability and operations layer: structured logs, metrics-like counters (in logs), and admin alerts via Telegram.

Execution model and concurrency:
1) The bot runs as a single process. Within it, manage a bounded concurrency pool for outbound work (RapidAPI calls and media downloads) to avoid saturating network/CPU.
2) Use an in-process scheduler loop to resume pending jobs after restarts by reading job records from MongoDB.
3) Deduplicate requests by TikTok video id and desired variant (e.g., no-watermark vs standard) so multiple users asking for the same video can reuse cached results.

State transitions for a download job:
1) created: received a message with a valid link, stored initial job record.
2) resolving: expanding URL and calling RapidAPI for metadata and download URLs.
3) downloading: fetching the actual media to temporary storage or preparing to stream.
4) uploading: sending the file to Telegram.
5) completed: stored result references (Telegram fileid, metadata) for reuse.
6) failed: stored error classification and user-visible message.

Failure handling strategy:
1) RapidAPI failures: retry a limited number of times with backoff; fail fast for 4xx that indicate invalid/unsupported content.
2) Download failures: retry on transient network issues; validate content-type and file size early.
3) Telegram upload failures: retry on transient Telegram errors; if file too large, fall back to sending a link or alternate lower-quality URL if available.

Storage strategy:
1) Temporary media: use simple filesystem storage (local temp directory) or an object storage abstraction for short-lived files.
2) Long-term reuse: prefer Telegram fileid caching in MongoDB so subsequent sends do not require re-downloading or re-uploading, subject to Telegram behavior and any observed invalidation.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust URLs directly. Only allow http/https, block local network IP ranges if you implement any direct fetching of user-provided URLs.
2) Keep RapidAPI key and Telegram token strictly in environment variables; do not log them.
3) Apply rate limiting per user and per chat; apply stricter limits in groups.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested):

1) users
Purpose: Track per-user settings, rate limits, and trust level.
Key fields:
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role: user, admin
4) counters: dailyRequests, lastRequestAt, violations
5) preferences: language, defaultMode (e.g., standard vs no-watermark if supported by API)

2) chats
Purpose: Track per-chat configuration and throttling.
Key fields:
1) telegramChatId (unique)
2) type: private, group, supergroup, channel
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) settings: allowAutoDownload (bool), requireCommandInGroups (bool)
5) counters: recentRequests window tracking

3) videocache
Purpose: Cache resolved TikTok metadata and Telegram file references for reuse.
Key fields:
1) canonicalVideoKey (unique) (derived from TikTok id when possible; fallback to normalized URL hash)
2) sourceUrlNormalized
3) resolvedAt, expiresAt (optional TTL)
4) metadata: description, author, duration, dimensions
5) variants: array of objects
   1) variantKey (e.g., standard, nowatermark, lowquality)
   2) downloadUrl (if allowed to cache; consider short TTL)
   3) sizeBytes (if known)
   4) telegramFileId (video/fileid)
   5) telegramMessageId sample (optional)
6) lastUsedAt, useCount

4) jobs
Purpose: Durable job tracking to survive restarts and provide user feedback.
Key fields:
1) jobId (unique)
2) status: created, resolving, downloading, uploading, completed, failed
3) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
4) inputUrl, normalizedUrl, canonicalVideoKey
5) requestedVariant
6) attempt counters: resolveAttempts, downloadAttempts, uploadAttempts
7) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt
8) error: code, message, stage, raw (sanitized)

5) adminevents (optional)
Purpose: Track noteworthy operational events.
Key fields:
1) createdAt
2) type: spike, rapidapidown, telegramerrors
3) details

Indexes and TTL:
1) videocache: index on canonicalVideoKey unique; optional TTL index on expiresAt.
2) jobs: index on status and updatedAt; optional TTL index on completedAt for cleanup.
3) users/chats: index on telegram ids.

In-memory maps (optional):
1) Short-lived dedupe locks keyed by canonicalVideoKey to avoid duplicate concurrent downloads.
2) Per-process rate limiter buckets for very fast checks, backed by MongoDB for durability if needed.

Data retention:
1) Store minimal personal data: Telegram IDs and operational counters.
2) Automatically purge old jobs and transient errors after a defined retention window.
3) Avoid storing raw TikTok download URLs long-term if they are short-lived; store only what is needed for caching and operations.

Key flows:
Flow A: User posts a TikTok link (happy path)
1) grammY receives message event.
2) Handler checks chat settings: in private chats auto-process; in groups optionally require command like /tiktok to reduce noise.
3) Extract URL(s). If multiple links, process the first or queue them with limits.
4) Normalize URL and resolve redirects for shortened links.
5) Create a job record in MongoDB and send an acknowledgement message (or reply) like Processing...
6) Check videocache by canonicalVideoKey.
7) If cached telegramFileId exists for chosen variant, send the video using fileid (fast path), mark job completed.
8) Otherwise call RapidAPI to get metadata and a downloadable video URL.
9) Validate response: presence of download URL, ensure it is http/https, optional HEAD request to estimate file size and content type.
10) If within Telegram limits, download to temporary storage (or stream if you choose) with size guardrails.
11) Upload/send video to the same chat. Capture returned telegram fileid.
12) Store telegram fileid into videocache variant, mark job completed, update counters.

Flow B: File too large or Telegram rejects upload
1) Detect size exceeds configured threshold (based on Telegram limits and your own safe margin).
2) Attempt alternate variant if RapidAPI provides multiple qualities.
3) If no suitable variant, send a message containing a direct link (and a warning that Telegram cannot upload the file). Optionally send metadata and instructions.
4) Mark job completed with outcome type deliveredaslink.

Flow C: RapidAPI error or unsupported link
1) RapidAPI returns error or cannot resolve content.
2) Classify error:
   1) user error: invalid URL, private video, region restriction.
   2) provider error: rate limit, outage, 5xx.
3) For user errors, reply with actionable guidance.
4) For provider errors, retry a limited number of times; if still failing, respond with a temporary failure message and ask user to retry later.
5) Mark job failed with sanitized error details.

Flow D: Rate limiting and abuse control
1) On every request, check per-user and per-chat quotas.
2) If exceeded, respond with a cooldown message and do not create a job (or create a rejected job for auditing).
3) For repeated violations, apply longer cooldowns.

Flow E: Restart recovery
1) On process start, query jobs in resolving/downloading/uploading state updated recently.
2) Re-enqueue them with a max retry limit.
3) For stale jobs beyond a threshold, mark failed and optionally notify user that the request timed out.

Flow F: Admin monitoring and controls
1) Admin uses /stats to see recent request counts, error rates, and cache hit rate.
2) On spikes or repeated provider failures, the bot sends an alert message to a configured admin chat.
3) Admin can /ban user (soft ban) or adjust group settings.

User experience details
1) Reply threading: always reply to the original message containing the link so the conversation is clear.
2) Progress updates: keep minimal to avoid spam in groups; optionally edit the acknowledgement message instead of sending multiple messages.
3) Idempotency: if user reposts the same link, reuse cache and respond quickly.

Tech stack:
Runtime and platform
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration. Token loaded from TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for durable storage of users, chats, jobs, and cache.
2) In-memory maps for fast short-lived locks and counters where safe, with MongoDB as source of truth for anything requiring restart persistence.

External dependencies
1) RapidAPI TikTok endpoint for resolving TikTok links into downloadable media URLs and metadata.
2) Native HTTP client (Node fetch or equivalent) for downloading media content and performing HEAD/GET requests with timeouts.

Storage
1) Temporary file handling via simple filesystem storage, with automatic cleanup.
2) Optional abstraction for object storage if you want temporary media off-disk; keep vendor-agnostic.

Deployment and operations
1) Run on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configuration via environment variables (Telegram token, RapidAPI key, MongoDB URI, admin chat id, limits).
3) Use long polling or webhook. Webhook requires an HTTPS endpoint; long polling is simpler operationally.

No additional infrastructure
1) No Redis, no separate queues, no additional databases.
2) Any background work remains in-process using controlled concurrency and MongoDB-backed job state.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability
1) Timeouts on all outbound requests: RapidAPI calls and media downloads.
2) Retries with exponential backoff for transient failures; strict caps to prevent infinite loops.
3) Circuit-breaker-like behavior: if RapidAPI fails repeatedly, temporarily degrade by replying with a helpful message and skipping new jobs for a short window to protect resources.

Performance
1) Concurrency limits for downloads/uploads to avoid memory spikes.
2) Cache-first strategy using Telegram fileid to reduce bandwidth and latency.
3) Prefer editing a single status message rather than sending multiple updates.

Scalability within single-process constraint
1) Keep a bounded worker pool for jobs.
2) Use MongoDB indexes and TTL cleanup to keep collections small.
3) Ensure per-request memory usage is bounded; stream downloads to disk where possible rather than buffering entire files.

Security and compliance
1) Input validation for URLs and strict allowlist of protocols.
2) Sanitize logs to avoid leaking user data beyond Telegram IDs and operational details.
3) Store minimal user data and define retention windows.
4) Consider content policy: provide a /privacy command describing what is stored.

Observability
1) Structured logging with request/job correlation ids.
2) Track key counters: requests per minute, cache hit rate, RapidAPI error rate, Telegram upload failures, average job duration.
3) Admin alerts sent to a configured admin chat when thresholds are exceeded.

Maintainability
1) Clear module boundaries: handlers, services (RapidAPI, downloader, telegram sender), persistence repositories, and configuration.
2) Centralized error classification to keep user messages consistent.
3) Feature flags in configuration for group behavior (auto-download vs command-only).

Testing strategy
1) Unit tests for URL normalization, rate limiting logic, and error classification.
2) Integration tests with mocked RapidAPI responses and Telegram API wrappers.
3) Manual test checklist for private chats, groups, multiple links, oversized videos, and restart recovery.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot, basic usage, and show current chat mode (auto vs command-only). (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Explain how to send TikTok links, supported link formats, and common troubleshooting steps. (usage: /help) [general]
• /tiktok — Explicitly download a TikTok link (useful in groups where auto-detection is disabled). (usage: /tiktok <tiktokurl>) [download]
• /mode — Set chat behavior: auto-download on link detection or command-only to reduce spam in groups. (usage: /mode auto | /mode command) [configuration]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job records, cached file references) and retention policy. (usage: /privacy) [general]
• /stats — Admin-only operational stats: request volume, error rates, cache hit rate, and queue depth. (usage: /stats) [admin]
• /ban — Admin-only: block a user from using the bot (soft ban) to mitigate abuse. (usage: /ban <telegramuserid>) [admin]
• /unban — Admin-only: remove a previously set ban. (usage: /unban <telegramuserid>) [admin]

.env.sample.content:
# Telegram
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=

# RapidAPI TikTok downloader
RAPIDAPI_KEY=
RAPIDAPI_HOST=

# Optional: base URL for your RapidAPI TikTok endpoint.
# If left as the placeholder (example.p.rapidapi.com), downloading is disabled and the bot will tell you to configure it.
RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE=https://example.p.rapidapi.com

# Optional controls
COOLDOWN_SECONDS=10
HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS=30000

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot downloads TikTok videos using a RapidAPI TikTok downloader API and sends the resulting video back into the same chat.

How to use
1) Start the bot with /start.
2) Paste a TikTok link into the chat. No command is required.
3) The bot will reply with the video. If Telegram upload fails, it will fall back to sending a document. If that fails too, it will send a direct download link.

Commands
1) /start
Shows a short welcome message and an example TikTok link.

2) /help
Shows supported URL formats, troubleshooting, and fallback behavior.

Supported TikTok URL formats
1) https://www.tiktok.com/...
2) https://tiktok.com/...
3) https://vm.tiktok.com/...
4) https://vt.tiktok.com/...

Rate limits
The bot applies a per-chat cooldown. By default this is 1 TikTok link per chat every 10 seconds. If you exceed it, the bot will ask you to wait.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Your Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) RAPIDAPI_KEY (required)
Your RapidAPI key.

3) RAPIDAPI_HOST (required)
The RapidAPI host for your selected TikTok downloader provider, for example: some-provider.p.rapidapi.com

4) RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE (optional but needed for downloads)
The base URL for the RapidAPI endpoint. This project defaults to https://example.p.rapidapi.com as a placeholder.
You must set it to the real provider base URL or downloading will be disabled.

5) COOLDOWN_SECONDS (optional)
Per-chat cooldown, default 10.

6) HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS (optional)
Timeout for RapidAPI calls and media downloads, default 30000.

Run locally
1) npm run install:root
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and fill it in
3) npm run dev

Deployment notes
This bot uses long polling and should run as a single Node.js service. Make sure all required environment variables are set in your hosting provider.

Troubleshooting
1) The bot replies that RapidAPI is not configured
Set RAPIDAPI_KEY, RAPIDAPI_HOST, and RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE.

2) Private or region-locked video
The downloader API may not be able to access it.

3) Telegram upload failed
The bot will automatically fall back to document, then to a direct link.

README.md.content:
TikTok Download Bot

This is a Telegram bot built with grammY that downloads TikTok videos using a RapidAPI TikTok downloader API and sends the video back into the same chat.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
npm run install:root

2) Configure env
Copy .env.sample to .env and set:
TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
RAPIDAPI_KEY
RAPIDAPI_HOST
RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE

3) Run
npm run dev

Deploy
1) Set the same environment variables in your hosting provider
2) Run build, then start

Docs
See DOCS.md

Created a new Telegram TikTok downloader bot using grammY. It auto-detects TikTok links, enforces a per-chat cooldown, calls your RapidAPI downloader endpoint, downloads the mp4 server-side, and sends it as video with fallbacks to document and then a direct link. Added production-safe logging, a runtime Bot Profile string, and DOCS.md plus a clean .env.sample for RapidAPI config.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram bot for downloading TikTok videos via RapidAPI and delivering them in chats

Summary:
Build a production-ready Telegram bot (single Node.js process, grammY) that accepts TikTok links, fetches downloadable video data via a RapidAPI TikTok endpoint, downloads the media server-side, and sends it back to the user or group chat. The system must be robust against Telegram file-size limits, TikTok/RapidAPI failures, duplicate requests, and abuse.

The bot will focus on a simple user experience (paste link, receive video) while implementing operational safeguards: request validation, rate limiting, deduplication, timeouts, retries with backoff, and observability. MongoDB is used for persistence (jobs, cache, users, quotas) and a simple filesystem storage or object storage abstraction is used for temporary media files when needed.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Users can paste a TikTok video URL in a private chat, group, or channel (where the bot is present) and receive the video file back in the same chat.

Core user expectations:
1) Minimal friction: no complex commands required; link detection should just work.
2) Fast feedback: immediate acknowledgement, progress messages when appropriate, and a clear success/failure outcome.
3) Reliability: handle intermittent RapidAPI failures, TikTok link variants, and Telegram upload issues.
4) Safety: protect the bot from abuse (spam, very high volume, repeated downloads) and from oversized files that cannot be sent.

Scope decisions to make it production-ready:
1) Supported inputs: standard TikTok share links, mobile links, and shortened URLs (resolve redirects).
2) Supported outputs: video file (preferred), fallback to sending a direct download link if file exceeds Telegram limits or download fails.
3) Chat contexts: private chats and groups by default; optional channel support where the bot has posting rights.
4) Localization: start with one language; design for future localization by centralizing user-facing strings.

Non-goals (initial release):
1) Full media editing (watermark removal variations beyond what the API provides).
2) Multi-tenant admin dashboard.
3) Heavy background processing pipelines or external queues (single-process constraint).

High-level architecture:
High-level components inside a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram update handling layer (grammY): receives messages, commands, and callback queries; routes them to handlers.
2) Input normalization and validation module: detects TikTok URLs, expands short links, extracts canonical video identifier where possible.
3) Job orchestration module: creates and tracks download jobs, enforces deduplication, and limits concurrency.
4) RapidAPI client module: calls the TikTok RapidAPI endpoint with timeouts, retries, and structured error handling.
5) Media download and delivery module: downloads the video to temporary storage or streams it (depending on size and Telegram constraints), then uploads/sends via Telegram API.
6) Persistence layer: MongoDB for users, quotas, job state, and cache of resolved TikTok metadata and file references.
7) Observability and operations layer: structured logs, metrics-like counters (in logs), and admin alerts via Telegram.

Execution model and concurrency:
1) The bot runs as a single process. Within it, manage a bounded concurrency pool for outbound work (RapidAPI calls and media downloads) to avoid saturating network/CPU.
2) Use an in-process scheduler loop to resume pending jobs after restarts by reading job records from MongoDB.
3) Deduplicate requests by TikTok video id and desired variant (e.g., no-watermark vs standard) so multiple users asking for the same video can reuse cached results.

State transitions for a download job:
1) created: received a message with a valid link, stored initial job record.
2) resolving: expanding URL and calling RapidAPI for metadata and download URLs.
3) downloading: fetching the actual media to temporary storage or preparing to stream.
4) uploading: sending the file to Telegram.
5) completed: stored result references (Telegram fileid, metadata) for reuse.
6) failed: stored error classification and user-visible message.

Failure handling strategy:
1) RapidAPI failures: retry a limited number of times with backoff; fail fast for 4xx that indicate invalid/unsupported content.
2) Download failures: retry on transient network issues; validate content-type and file size early.
3) Telegram upload failures: retry on transient Telegram errors; if file too large, fall back to sending a link or alternate lower-quality URL if available.

Storage strategy:
1) Temporary media: use simple filesystem storage (local temp directory) or an object storage abstraction for short-lived files.
2) Long-term reuse: prefer Telegram fileid caching in MongoDB so subsequent sends do not require re-downloading or re-uploading, subject to Telegram behavior and any observed invalidation.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust URLs directly. Only allow http/https, block local network IP ranges if you implement any direct fetching of user-provided URLs.
2) Keep RapidAPI key and Telegram token strictly in environment variables; do not log them.
3) Apply rate limiting per user and per chat; apply stricter limits in groups.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested):

1) users
Purpose: Track per-user settings, rate limits, and trust level.
Key fields:
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role: user, admin
4) counters: dailyRequests, lastRequestAt, violations
5) preferences: language, defaultMode (e.g., standard vs no-watermark if supported by API)

2) chats
Purpose: Track per-chat configuration and throttling.
Key fields:
1) telegramChatId (unique)
2) type: private, group, supergroup, channel
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) settings: allowAutoDownload (bool), requireCommandInGroups (bool)
5) counters: recentRequests window tracking

3) videocache
Purpose: Cache resolved TikTok metadata and Telegram file references for reuse.
Key fields:
1) canonicalVideoKey (unique) (derived from TikTok id when possible; fallback to normalized URL hash)
2) sourceUrlNormalized
3) resolvedAt, expiresAt (optional TTL)
4) metadata: description, author, duration, dimensions
5) variants: array of objects
   1) variantKey (e.g., standard, nowatermark, lowquality)
   2) downloadUrl (if allowed to cache; consider short TTL)
   3) sizeBytes (if known)
   4) telegramFileId (video/fileid)
   5) telegramMessageId sample (optional)
6) lastUsedAt, useCount

4) jobs
Purpose: Durable job tracking to survive restarts and provide user feedback.
Key fields:
1) jobId (unique)
2) status: created, resolving, downloading, uploading, completed, failed
3) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
4) inputUrl, normalizedUrl, canonicalVideoKey
5) requestedVariant
6) attempt counters: resolveAttempts, downloadAttempts, uploadAttempts
7) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt
8) error: code, message, stage, raw (sanitized)

5) adminevents (optional)
Purpose: Track noteworthy operational events.
Key fields:
1) createdAt
2) type: spike, rapidapidown, telegramerrors
3) details

Indexes and TTL:
1) videocache: index on canonicalVideoKey unique; optional TTL index on expiresAt.
2) jobs: index on status and updatedAt; optional TTL index on completedAt for cleanup.
3) users/chats: index on telegram ids.

In-memory maps (optional):
1) Short-lived dedupe locks keyed by canonicalVideoKey to avoid duplicate concurrent downloads.
2) Per-process rate limiter buckets for very fast checks, backed by MongoDB for durability if needed.

Data retention:
1) Store minimal personal data: Telegram IDs and operational counters.
2) Automatically purge old jobs and transient errors after a defined retention window.
3) Avoid storing raw TikTok download URLs long-term if they are short-lived; store only what is needed for caching and operations.

Key flows:
Flow A: User posts a TikTok link (happy path)
1) grammY receives message event.
2) Handler checks chat settings: in private chats auto-process; in groups optionally require command like /tiktok to reduce noise.
3) Extract URL(s). If multiple links, process the first or queue them with limits.
4) Normalize URL and resolve redirects for shortened links.
5) Create a job record in MongoDB and send an acknowledgement message (or reply) like Processing...
6) Check videocache by canonicalVideoKey.
7) If cached telegramFileId exists for chosen variant, send the video using fileid (fast path), mark job completed.
8) Otherwise call RapidAPI to get metadata and a downloadable video URL.
9) Validate response: presence of download URL, ensure it is http/https, optional HEAD request to estimate file size and content type.
10) If within Telegram limits, download to temporary storage (or stream if you choose) with size guardrails.
11) Upload/send video to the same chat. Capture returned telegram fileid.
12) Store telegram fileid into videocache variant, mark job completed, update counters.

Flow B: File too large or Telegram rejects upload
1) Detect size exceeds configured threshold (based on Telegram limits and your own safe margin).
2) Attempt alternate variant if RapidAPI provides multiple qualities.
3) If no suitable variant, send a message containing a direct link (and a warning that Telegram cannot upload the file). Optionally send metadata and instructions.
4) Mark job completed with outcome type deliveredaslink.

Flow C: RapidAPI error or unsupported link
1) RapidAPI returns error or cannot resolve content.
2) Classify error:
   1) user error: invalid URL, private video, region restriction.
   2) provider error: rate limit, outage, 5xx.
3) For user errors, reply with actionable guidance.
4) For provider errors, retry a limited number of times; if still failing, respond with a temporary failure message and ask user to retry later.
5) Mark job failed with sanitized error details.

Flow D: Rate limiting and abuse control
1) On every request, check per-user and per-chat quotas.
2) If exceeded, respond with a cooldown message and do not create a job (or create a rejected job for auditing).
3) For repeated violations, apply longer cooldowns.

Flow E: Restart recovery
1) On process start, query jobs in resolving/downloading/uploading state updated recently.
2) Re-enqueue them with a max retry limit.
3) For stale jobs beyond a threshold, mark failed and optionally notify user that the request timed out.

Flow F: Admin monitoring and controls
1) Admin uses /stats to see recent request counts, error rates, and cache hit rate.
2) On spikes or repeated provider failures, the bot sends an alert message to a configured admin chat.
3) Admin can /ban user (soft ban) or adjust group settings.

User experience details
1) Reply threading: always reply to the original message containing the link so the conversation is clear.
2) Progress updates: keep minimal to avoid spam in groups; optionally edit the acknowledgement message instead of sending multiple messages.
3) Idempotency: if user reposts the same link, reuse cache and respond quickly.

Tech stack:
Runtime and platform
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration. Token loaded from TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for durable storage of users, chats, jobs, and cache.
2) In-memory maps for fast short-lived locks and counters where safe, with MongoDB as source of truth for anything requiring restart persistence.

External dependencies
1) RapidAPI TikTok endpoint for resolving TikTok links into downloadable media URLs and metadata.
2) Native HTTP client (Node fetch or equivalent) for downloading media content and performing HEAD/GET requests with timeouts.

Storage
1) Temporary file handling via simple filesystem storage, with automatic cleanup.
2) Optional abstraction for object storage if you want temporary media off-disk; keep vendor-agnostic.

Deployment and operations
1) Run on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configuration via environment variables (Telegram token, RapidAPI key, MongoDB URI, admin chat id, limits).
3) Use long polling or webhook. Webhook requires an HTTPS endpoint; long polling is simpler operationally.

No additional infrastructure
1) No Redis, no separate queues, no additional databases.
2) Any background work remains in-process using controlled concurrency and MongoDB-backed job state.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability
1) Timeouts on all outbound requests: RapidAPI calls and media downloads.
2) Retries with exponential backoff for transient failures; strict caps to prevent infinite loops.
3) Circuit-breaker-like behavior: if RapidAPI fails repeatedly, temporarily degrade by replying with a helpful message and skipping new jobs for a short window to protect resources.

Performance
1) Concurrency limits for downloads/uploads to avoid memory spikes.
2) Cache-first strategy using Telegram fileid to reduce bandwidth and latency.
3) Prefer editing a single status message rather than sending multiple updates.

Scalability within single-process constraint
1) Keep a bounded worker pool for jobs.
2) Use MongoDB indexes and TTL cleanup to keep collections small.
3) Ensure per-request memory usage is bounded; stream downloads to disk where possible rather than buffering entire files.

Security and compliance
1) Input validation for URLs and strict allowlist of protocols.
2) Sanitize logs to avoid leaking user data beyond Telegram IDs and operational details.
3) Store minimal user data and define retention windows.
4) Consider content policy: provide a /privacy command describing what is stored.

Observability
1) Structured logging with request/job correlation ids.
2) Track key counters: requests per minute, cache hit rate, RapidAPI error rate, Telegram upload failures, average job duration.
3) Admin alerts sent to a configured admin chat when thresholds are exceeded.

Maintainability
1) Clear module boundaries: handlers, services (RapidAPI, downloader, telegram sender), persistence repositories, and configuration.
2) Centralized error classification to keep user messages consistent.
3) Feature flags in configuration for group behavior (auto-download vs command-only).

Testing strategy
1) Unit tests for URL normalization, rate limiting logic, and error classification.
2) Integration tests with mocked RapidAPI responses and Telegram API wrappers.
3) Manual test checklist for private chats, groups, multiple links, oversized videos, and restart recovery.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduce the bot, basic usage, and show current chat mode (auto vs command-only). (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Explain how to send TikTok links, supported link formats, and common troubleshooting steps. (usage: /help) [general]
• /tiktok — Explicitly download a TikTok link (useful in groups where auto-detection is disabled). (usage: /tiktok <tiktokurl>) [download]
• /mode — Set chat behavior: auto-download on link detection or command-only to reduce spam in groups. (usage: /mode auto | /mode command) [configuration]
• /privacy — Explain what data is stored (Telegram IDs, job records, cached file references) and retention policy. (usage: /privacy) [general]
• /stats — Admin-only operational stats: request volume, error rates, cache hit rate, and queue depth. (usage: /stats) [admin]
• /ban — Admin-only: block a user from using the bot (soft ban) to mitigate abuse. (usage: /ban <telegramuserid>) [admin]
• /unban — Admin-only: remove a previously set ban. (usage: /unban <telegramuserid>) [admin]

Flow B: File too large or Telegram rejects upload
1) Detect size exceeds configured threshold (based on Telegram limits and your own safe margin).
2) Attempt alternate variant if RapidAPI provides multiple qualities.
3) If no suitable variant, send a message containing a direct link (and a warning that Telegram cannot upload the file). Optionally send metadata and instructions.
4) Mark job completed with outcome type deliveredaslink.

Flow C: RapidAPI error or unsupported link
1) RapidAPI returns error or cannot resolve content.
2) Classify error:
   1) user error: invalid URL, private video, region restriction.
   2) provider error: rate limit, outage, 5xx.
3) For user errors, reply with actionable guidance.
4) For provider errors, retry a limited number of times; if still failing, respond with a temporary failure message and ask user to retry later.
5) Mark job failed with sanitized error details.

Flow D: Rate limiting and abuse control
1) On every request, check per-user and per-chat quotas.
2) If exceeded, respond with a cooldown message and do not create a job (or create a rejected job for auditing).
3) For repeated violations, apply longer cooldowns.

Flow E: Restart recovery
1) On process start, query jobs in resolving/downloading/uploading state updated recently.
2) Re-enqueue them with a max retry limit.
3) For stale jobs beyond a threshold, mark failed and optionally notify user that the request timed out.

Flow F: Admin monitoring and controls
1) Admin uses /stats to see recent request counts, error rates, and cache hit rate.
2) On spikes or repeated provider failures, the bot sends an alert message to a configured admin chat.
3) Admin can /ban user (soft ban) or adjust group settings.

User experience details
1) Reply threading: always reply to the original message containing the link so the conversation is clear.
2) Progress updates: keep minimal to avoid spam in groups; optionally edit the acknowledgement message instead of sending multiple messages.
3) Idempotency: if user reposts the same link, reuse cache and respond quickly.

DOCS.md.content:
What this bot does
This Telegram bot downloads TikTok videos using a RapidAPI TikTok downloader API and sends the resulting video back into the same chat.

How to use
1) Start the bot with /start.
2) Paste a TikTok link into the chat. No command is required.
3) The bot will reply with the video. If Telegram upload fails, it will fall back to sending a document. If that fails too, it will send a direct download link.

Commands
1) /start
Shows a short welcome message and an example TikTok link.

2) /help
Shows supported URL formats, troubleshooting, and fallback behavior.

Supported TikTok URL formats
1) https://www.tiktok.com/...
2) https://tiktok.com/...
3) https://vm.tiktok.com/...
4) https://vt.tiktok.com/...

Rate limits
The bot applies a per-chat cooldown. By default this is 1 TikTok link per chat every 10 seconds. If you exceed it, the bot will ask you to wait.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Your Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) RAPIDAPI_KEY (required)
Your RapidAPI key.

3) RAPIDAPI_HOST (required)
The RapidAPI host for your selected TikTok downloader provider, for example: some-provider.p.rapidapi.com

4) RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE (optional but needed for downloads)
The base URL for the RapidAPI endpoint. This project defaults to https://example.p.rapidapi.com as a placeholder.
You must set it to the real provider base URL or downloading will be disabled.

5) COOLDOWN_SECONDS (optional)
Per-chat cooldown, default 10.

6) HTTP_TIMEOUT_MS (optional)
Timeout for RapidAPI calls and media downloads, default 30000.

Run locally
1) npm run install:root
2) Copy .env.sample to .env and fill it in
3) npm run dev

Deployment notes
This bot uses long polling and should run as a single Node.js service. Make sure all required environment variables are set in your hosting provider.

Troubleshooting
1) The bot replies that RapidAPI is not configured
Set RAPIDAPI_KEY, RAPIDAPI_HOST, and RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE.

2) Private or region-locked video
The downloader API may not be able to access it.

3) Telegram upload failed
The bot will automatically fall back to document, then to a direct link.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram bot for downloading TikTok videos via RapidAPI and delivering them in chats

Summary:
Build a production-ready Telegram bot (single Node.js process, grammY) that accepts TikTok links, fetches downloadable video data via a RapidAPI TikTok endpoint, downloads the media server-side, and sends it back to the user or group chat. The system must be robust against Telegram file-size limits, TikTok/RapidAPI failures, duplicate requests, and abuse.

The bot will focus on a simple user experience (paste link, receive video) while implementing operational safeguards: request validation, rate limiting, deduplication, timeouts, retries with backoff, and observability. MongoDB is used for persistence (jobs, cache, users, quotas) and a simple filesystem storage or object storage abstraction is used for temporary media files when needed.

Product goals:
Primary goal: Users can paste a TikTok video URL in a private chat, group, or channel (where the bot is present) and receive the video file back in the same chat.

Core user expectations:
1) Minimal friction: no complex commands required; link detection should just work.
2) Fast feedback: immediate acknowledgement, progress messages when appropriate, and a clear success/failure outcome.
3) Reliability: handle intermittent RapidAPI failures, TikTok link variants, and Telegram upload issues.
4) Safety: protect the bot from abuse (spam, very high volume, repeated downloads) and from oversized files that cannot be sent.

Scope decisions to make it production-ready:
1) Supported inputs: standard TikTok share links, mobile links, and shortened URLs (resolve redirects).
2) Supported outputs: video file (preferred), fallback to sending a direct download link if file exceeds Telegram limits or download fails.
3) Chat contexts: private chats and groups by default; optional channel support where the bot has posting rights.
4) Localization: start with one language; design for future localization by centralizing user-facing strings.

Non-goals (initial release):
1) Full media editing (watermark removal variations beyond what the API provides).
2) Multi-tenant admin dashboard.
3) Heavy background processing pipelines or external queues (single-process constraint).

High-level architecture:
High-level components inside a single Node.js service:
1) Telegram update handling layer (grammY): receives messages, commands, and callback queries; routes them to handlers.
2) Input normalization and validation module: detects TikTok URLs, expands short links, extracts canonical video identifier where possible.
3) Job orchestration module: creates and tracks download jobs, enforces deduplication, and limits concurrency.
4) RapidAPI client module: calls the TikTok RapidAPI endpoint with timeouts, retries, and structured error handling.
5) Media download and delivery module: downloads the video to temporary storage or streams it (depending on size and Telegram constraints), then uploads/sends via Telegram API.
6) Persistence layer: MongoDB for users, quotas, job state, and cache of resolved TikTok metadata and file references.
7) Observability and operations layer: structured logs, metrics-like counters (in logs), and admin alerts via Telegram.

Execution model and concurrency:
1) The bot runs as a single process. Within it, manage a bounded concurrency pool for outbound work (RapidAPI calls and media downloads) to avoid saturating network/CPU.
2) Use an in-process scheduler loop to resume pending jobs after restarts by reading job records from MongoDB.
3) Deduplicate requests by TikTok video id and desired variant (e.g., no-watermark vs standard) so multiple users asking for the same video can reuse cached results.

State transitions for a download job:
1) created: received a message with a valid link, stored initial job record.
2) resolving: expanding URL and calling RapidAPI for metadata and download URLs.
3) downloading: fetching the actual media to temporary storage or preparing to stream.
4) uploading: sending the file to Telegram.
5) completed: stored result references (Telegram fileid, metadata) for reuse.
6) failed: stored error classification and user-visible message.

Failure handling strategy:
1) RapidAPI failures: retry a limited number of times with backoff; fail fast for 4xx that indicate invalid/unsupported content.
2) Download failures: retry on transient network issues; validate content-type and file size early.
3) Telegram upload failures: retry on transient Telegram errors; if file too large, fall back to sending a link or alternate lower-quality URL if available.

Storage strategy:
1) Temporary media: use simple filesystem storage (local temp directory) or an object storage abstraction for short-lived files.
2) Long-term reuse: prefer Telegram fileid caching in MongoDB so subsequent sends do not require re-downloading or re-uploading, subject to Telegram behavior and any observed invalidation.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust URLs directly. Only allow http/https, block local network IP ranges if you implement any direct fetching of user-provided URLs.
2) Keep RapidAPI key and Telegram token strictly in environment variables; do not log them.
3) Apply rate limiting per user and per chat; apply stricter limits in groups.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested):

1) users
Purpose: Track per-user settings, rate limits, and trust level.
Key fields:
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role: user, admin
4) counters: dailyRequests, lastRequestAt, violations
5) preferences: language, defaultMode (e.g., standard vs no-watermark if supported by API)

2) chats
Purpose: Track per-chat configuration and throttling.
Key fields:
1) telegramChatId (unique)
2) type: private, group, supergroup, channel
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) settings: allowAutoDownload (bool), requireCommandInGroups (bool)
5) counters: recentRequests window tracking

3) videocache
Purpose: Cache resolved TikTok metadata and Telegram file references for reuse.
Key fields:
1) canonicalVideoKey (unique) (derived from TikTok id when possible; fallback to normalized URL hash)
2) sourceUrlNormalized
3) resolvedAt, expiresAt (optional TTL)
4) metadata: description, author, duration, dimensions
5) variants: array of objects
   1) variantKey (e.g., standard, nowatermark, lowquality)
   2) downloadUrl (if allowed to cache; consider short TTL)
   3) sizeBytes (if known)
   4) telegramFileId (video/fileid)
   5) telegramMessageId sample (optional)
6) lastUsedAt, useCount

4) jobs
Purpose: Durable job tracking to survive restarts and provide user feedback.
Key fields:
1) jobId (unique)
2) status: created, resolving, downloading, uploading, completed, failed
3) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
4) inputUrl, normalizedUrl, canonicalVideoKey
5) requestedVariant
6) attempt counters: resolveAttempts, downloadAttempts, uploadAttempts
7) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt
8) error: code, message, stage, raw (sanitized)

5) adminevents (optional)
Purpose: Track noteworthy operational events.
Key fields:
1) createdAt
2) type: spike, rapidapidown, telegramerrors
3) details

Indexes and TTL:
1) videocache: index on canonicalVideoKey unique; optional TTL index on expiresAt.
2) jobs: index on status and updatedAt; optional TTL index on completedAt for cleanup.
3) users/chats: index on telegram ids.

In-memory maps (optional):
1) Short-lived dedupe locks keyed by canonicalVideoKey to avoid duplicate concurrent downloads.
2) Per-process rate limiter buckets for very fast checks, backed by MongoDB for durability if needed.

Data retention:
1) Store minimal personal data: Telegram IDs and operational counters.
2) Automatically purge old jobs and transient errors after a defined retention window.
3) Avoid storing raw TikTok download URLs long-term if they are short-lived; store only what is needed for caching and operations.

Key flows:
Flow A: User posts a TikTok link (happy path)
1) grammY receives message event.
2) Handler checks chat settings: in private chats auto-process; in groups optionally require command like /tiktok to reduce noise.
3) Extract URL(s). If multiple links, process the first or queue them with limits.
4) Normalize URL and resolve redirects for shortened links.
5) Create a job record in MongoDB and send an acknowledgement message (or reply) like Processing...
6) Check videocache by canonicalVideoKey.
7) If cached telegramFileId exists for chosen variant, send the video using fileid (fast path), mark job completed.
8) Otherwise call RapidAPI to get metadata and a downloadable video URL.
9) Validate response: presence of download URL, ensure it is http/https, optional HEAD request to estimate file size and content type.
10) If within Telegram limits, download to temporary storage (or stream if you choose) with size guardrails.
11) Upload/send video to the same chat. Capture returned telegram fileid.
12) Store telegram fileid into videocache variant, mark job completed, update counters.

1) Detect size exceeds configured threshold (based on Telegram limits and your own safe margin).
2) Attempt alternate variant if RapidAPI provides multiple qualities.

4) Mark job completed with outcome type deliveredaslink.

4) RapidAPI client module: calls the TikTok RapidAPI endpoint with timeouts, retries, and structured error handling.
5) Media download and delivery module: downloads the video to temporary storage or streams it (depending on size and Telegram constraints), then uploads/sends via Telegram API.
6) Persistence layer: MongoDB for users, quotas, job state, and cache of resolved TikTok metadata and file references.
7) Observability and operations layer: structured logs, metrics-like counters (in logs), and admin alerts via Telegram.

Execution model and concurrency:
1) The bot runs as a single process. Within it, manage a bounded concurrency pool for outbound work (RapidAPI calls and media downloads) to avoid saturating network/CPU.
2) Use an in-process scheduler loop to resume pending jobs after restarts by reading job records from MongoDB.
3) Deduplicate requests by TikTok video id and desired variant (e.g., no-watermark vs standard) so multiple users asking for the same video can reuse cached results.

State transitions for a download job:
1) created: received a message with a valid link, stored initial job record.
2) resolving: expanding URL and calling RapidAPI for metadata and download URLs.
3) downloading: fetching the actual media to temporary storage or preparing to stream.
4) uploading: sending the file to Telegram.
5) completed: stored result references (Telegram fileid, metadata) for reuse.
6) failed: stored error classification and user-visible message.

Failure handling strategy:
1) RapidAPI failures: retry a limited number of times with backoff; fail fast for 4xx that indicate invalid/unsupported content.
2) Download failures: retry on transient network issues; validate content-type and file size early.
3) Telegram upload failures: retry on transient Telegram errors; if file too large, fall back to sending a link or alternate lower-quality URL if available.

Storage strategy:
1) Temporary media: use simple filesystem storage (local temp directory) or an object storage abstraction for short-lived files.
2) Long-term reuse: prefer Telegram fileid caching in MongoDB so subsequent sends do not require re-downloading or re-uploading, subject to Telegram behavior and any observed invalidation.

Security boundaries:
1) Never trust URLs directly. Only allow http/https, block local network IP ranges if you implement any direct fetching of user-provided URLs.
2) Keep RapidAPI key and Telegram token strictly in environment variables; do not log them.
3) Apply rate limiting per user and per chat; apply stricter limits in groups.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB collections (suggested):

1) users
Purpose: Track per-user settings, rate limits, and trust level.
Key fields:
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) createdAt, lastSeenAt
3) role: user, admin
4) counters: dailyRequests, lastRequestAt, violations
5) preferences: language, defaultMode (e.g., standard vs no-watermark if supported by API)

2) chats
Purpose: Track per-chat configuration and throttling.
Key fields:
1) telegramChatId (unique)
2) type: private, group, supergroup, channel
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) settings: allowAutoDownload (bool), requireCommandInGroups (bool)
5) counters: recentRequests window tracking

3) videocache
Purpose: Cache resolved TikTok metadata and Telegram file references for reuse.
Key fields:
1) canonicalVideoKey (unique) (derived from TikTok id when possible; fallback to normalized URL hash)
2) sourceUrlNormalized
3) resolvedAt, expiresAt (optional TTL)
4) metadata: description, author, duration, dimensions
5) variants: array of objects
   1) variantKey (e.g., standard, nowatermark, lowquality)
   2) downloadUrl (if allowed to cache; consider short TTL)
   3) sizeBytes (if known)
   4) telegramFileId (video/fileid)
   5) telegramMessageId sample (optional)
6) lastUsedAt, useCount

4) jobs
Purpose: Durable job tracking to survive restarts and provide user feedback.
Key fields:
1) jobId (unique)
2) status: created, resolving, downloading, uploading, completed, failed
3) telegramChatId, telegramMessageId, telegramUserId
4) inputUrl, normalizedUrl, canonicalVideoKey
5) requestedVariant
6) attempt counters: resolveAttempts, downloadAttempts, uploadAttempts
7) timestamps: createdAt, updatedAt, completedAt
8) error: code, message, stage, raw (sanitized)

5) adminevents (optional)
Purpose: Track noteworthy operational events.
Key fields:
1) createdAt
2) type: spike, rapidapidown, telegramerrors
3) details

Indexes and TTL:
1) videocache: index on canonicalVideoKey unique; optional TTL index on expiresAt.
2) jobs: index on status and updatedAt; optional TTL index on completedAt for cleanup.
3) users/chats: index on telegram ids.

In-memory maps (optional):
1) Short-lived dedupe locks keyed by canonicalVideoKey to avoid duplicate concurrent downloads.
2) Per-process rate limiter buckets for very fast checks, backed by MongoDB for durability if needed.

Data retention:
1) Store minimal personal data: Telegram IDs and operational counters.
2) Automatically purge old jobs and transient errors after a defined retention window.
3) Avoid storing raw TikTok download URLs long-term if they are short-lived; store only what is needed for caching and operations.
planSections.keyFlows:
Flow A: User posts a TikTok link (happy path)
1) grammY receives message event.
2) Handler checks chat settings: in private chats auto-process; in groups optionally require command like /tiktok to reduce noise.
3) Extract URL(s). If multiple links, process the first or queue them with limits.
4) Normalize URL and resolve redirects for shortened links.
5) Create a job record in MongoDB and send an acknowledgement message (or reply) like Processing...
6) Check videocache by canonicalVideoKey.
7) If cached telegramFileId exists for chosen variant, send the video using fileid (fast path), mark job completed.
8) Otherwise call RapidAPI to get metadata and a downloadable video URL.
9) Validate response: presence of download URL, ensure it is http/https, optional HEAD request to estimate file size and content type.
10) If within Telegram limits, download to temporary storage (or stream if you choose) with size guardrails.
11) Upload/send video to the same chat. Capture returned telegram fileid.
12) Store telegram fileid into videocache variant, mark job completed, update counters.

Flow B: File too large or Telegram rejects upload
1) Detect size exceeds configured threshold (based on Telegram limits and your own safe margin).
2) Attempt alternate variant if RapidAPI provides multiple qualities.
3) If no suitable variant, send a message containing a direct link (and a warning that Telegram cannot upload the file). Optionally send metadata and instructions.
4) Mark job completed with outcome type deliveredaslink.

Flow C: RapidAPI error or unsupported link
1) RapidAPI returns error or cannot resolve content.
2) Classify error:
   1) user error: invalid URL, private video, region restriction.
   2) provider error: rate limit, outage, 5xx.
3) For user errors, reply with actionable guidance.
4) For provider errors, retry a limited number of times; if still failing, respond with a temporary failure message and ask user to retry later.
5) Mark job failed with sanitized error details.

Flow D: Rate limiting and abuse control
1) On every request, check per-user and per-chat quotas.
2) If exceeded, respond with a cooldown message and do not create a job (or create a rejected job for auditing).
3) For repeated violations, apply longer cooldowns.

Flow E: Restart recovery
1) On process start, query jobs in resolving/downloading/uploading state updated recently.
2) Re-enqueue them with a max retry limit.
3) For stale jobs beyond a threshold, mark failed and optionally notify user that the request timed out.

Flow F: Admin monitoring and controls
1) Admin uses /stats to see recent request counts, error rates, and cache hit rate.
2) On spikes or repeated provider failures, the bot sends an alert message to a configured admin chat.
3) Admin can /ban user (soft ban) or adjust group settings.

User experience details
1) Reply threading: always reply to the original message containing the link so the conversation is clear.
2) Progress updates: keep minimal to avoid spam in groups; optionally edit the acknowledgement message instead of sending multiple messages.
3) Idempotency: if user reposts the same link, reuse cache and respond quickly.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and platform
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration. Token loaded from TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN environment variable.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for durable storage of users, chats, jobs, and cache.
2) In-memory maps for fast short-lived locks and counters where safe, with MongoDB as source of truth for anything requiring restart persistence.

External dependencies
1) RapidAPI TikTok endpoint for resolving TikTok links into downloadable media URLs and metadata.
2) Native HTTP client (Node fetch or equivalent) for downloading media content and performing HEAD/GET requests with timeouts.

Storage
1) Temporary file handling via simple filesystem storage, with automatic cleanup.
2) Optional abstraction for object storage if you want temporary media off-disk; keep vendor-agnostic.

Deployment and operations
1) Run on a generic Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configuration via environment variables (Telegram token, RapidAPI key, MongoDB URI, admin chat id, limits).
3) Use long polling or webhook. Webhook requires an HTTPS endpoint; long polling is simpler operationally.

No additional infrastructure
1) No Redis, no separate queues, no additional databases.
2) Any background work remains in-process using controlled concurrency and MongoDB-backed job state.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability
1) Timeouts on all outbound requests: RapidAPI calls and media downloads.
2) Retries with exponential backoff for transient failures; strict caps to prevent infinite loops.
3) Circuit-breaker-like behavior: if RapidAPI fails repeatedly, temporarily degrade by replying with a helpful message and skipping new jobs for a short window to protect resources.

Performance
1) Concurrency limits for downloads/uploads to avoid memory spikes.
2) Cache-first strategy using Telegram fileid to reduce bandwidth and latency.
3) Prefer editing a single status message rather than sending multiple updates.

Scalability within single-process constraint
1) Keep a bounded worker pool for jobs.
2) Use MongoDB indexes and TTL cleanup to keep collections small.
3) Ensure per-request memory usage is bounded; stream downloads to disk where possible rather than buffering entire files.

Security and compliance
1) Input validation for URLs and strict allowlist of protocols.
2) Sanitize logs to avoid leaking user data beyond Telegram IDs and operational details.
3) Store minimal user data and define retention windows.
4) Consider content policy: provide a /privacy command describing what is stored.

Observability
1) Structured logging with request/job correlation ids.
2) Track key counters: requests per minute, cache hit rate, RapidAPI error rate, Telegram upload failures, average job duration.
3) Admin alerts sent to a configured admin chat when thresholds are exceeded.

Maintainability
1) Clear module boundaries: handlers, services (RapidAPI, downloader, telegram sender), persistence repositories, and configuration.
2) Centralized error classification to keep user messages consistent.
3) Feature flags in configuration for group behavior (auto-download vs command-only).

Testing strategy
1) Unit tests for URL normalization, rate limiting logic, and error classification.
2) Integration tests with mocked RapidAPI responses and Telegram API wrappers.
3) Manual test checklist for private chats, groups, multiple links, oversized videos, and restart recovery.

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Token for your Telegram bot from BotFather.
envVarDocs.usage: Used to start the grammY bot.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with @BotFather and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: RapidAPI Key
envVarDocs.description: RapidAPI key used to authenticate requests to the TikTok downloader API.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get it from your RapidAPI account dashboard.

envVarDocs.title: RapidAPI Host
envVarDocs.description: The RapidAPI host for your chosen TikTok downloader provider (provider-specific).
envVarDocs.howToGet: Shown in the RapidAPI provider docs for the endpoint you selected.

envVarDocs.title: RapidAPI TikTok Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for the TikTok downloader endpoint. If left as the placeholder, downloading is disabled and the bot returns a clear configuration error.
envVarDocs.usage: The service calls GET {RAPIDAPI_TIKTOK_ENDPOINT_BASE}/ with query param url=<tiktokUrl>.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Copy the base URL shown in your RapidAPI endpoint docs.

envVarDocs.title: Chat Cooldown Seconds
envVarDocs.description: Per-chat cooldown to prevent spam. Default is 10 seconds.
envVarDocs.usage: If a new TikTok link arrives before the cooldown expires, the bot replies with a wait message.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set any integer value you want.

envVarDocs.title: HTTP Timeout
envVarDocs.description: Timeout for RapidAPI calls and media downloads. Default is 30000 ms.
envVarDocs.usage: Applied to RapidAPI requests and the mp4 download request.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set any integer value you want.
